questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Recently, the Centre classified limestone entirely as a major mineral through a gazette notification,** removing the earlier distinction based on end use. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ The order was issued under Section 20A of the Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Act (MMDR Act). ○ This move aims to simplify business operations and promote ease of doing business by eliminating end-use restrictions for leaseholders.
● Classification of limestone as a major mineral implies that **leaseholders of limestone can now sell or** **utilize it for any purpose without regulatory restrictions.**
○ This enables limestone minor mineral leaseholders to freely sell limestone to cement industries as well as to the other industries. T**he leaseholders will be able to sell or utilise limestone for any** **purpose without any end-use restrictions** based on artificial regulatory distinction of minor or major mineral. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Previously, limestone was classified as a minor mineral only when used in kilns to manufacture lime for building materials. While for all other uses—such as in cement production, steel, chemicals, fertilizers, and other industries—it was categorized as a major mineral.
○ The Centre has now classified limestone entirely as a major mineral, ending the dual categorisation based on end-use. The Government has allowed the conversion of existing minor mineral limestone leases into major mineral leases. The existing minor mineral leases of limestone will continue to operate as major mineral leases without any disruption.
● Earlier in February 2025, the Ministry of Mines had reclassified minor minerals Barytes, Felspar, Mica, and Quartz as major minerals. **So, Both statement I and statement II are correct and statement II explains statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Minor minerals are defined under the Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) (MMDR) Act, 1957, as building stones, gravel, ordinary clay, and ordinary sand. The term ‘minor minerals’ is often understood to imply a lesser quantity of significance.
● However, in the Quarry Owners Association vs State of Bihar (2000) case, the Supreme Court clarified that the **classification of minerals as major or minor under the MMDR Act is** **determined by their end use and local importance**, rather than by their volume, availability or production levels.
● The classification is also for the purpose of administrative convenience: while the **Central** **Government retains control over major minerals** with national and strategic relevance, like coal, iron ore or bauxite, t**he regulation and management of minor minerals fall under the purview of** **state governments.**
● The Central Government also has the authority to declare any other mineral to be a minor mineral. It has thus far notified around 31 minerals as minor minerals such as Gypsum, Mica, Quartz, Claybased minerals, Sand, etc.
**Key facts about Limestone:**
● Limestone is a sedimentary rock composed principally of calcium carbonate (calcite) or the double carbonate of calcium and magnesium (dolomite).
● It is usually gray, but it may also be white, yellow or brown.
● Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, and Andhra Pradesh together account for 50% of limestone production in India.
**Relevance:** Recently, the central government classified limestone entirely as a major mineral through a gazette notification, removing the earlier distinction based on end use.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText: What is Maitri-II, recently seen in the news?

options.a:
It is India’s first automated and remotely
operated research base in Antarctica.
options.b:
It is a newly launched coastal surveillance
radar system deployed by the Indian Navy.
options.c:
It is a high-capacity electric train project
being developed under Indian Railways.
options.d:
It is ISRO’s next-generation Earth-observation
satellite designed for climate monitoring.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● India has proposed to establish **a new research station in Antarctica, named Maitri-2**, to replace its aging predecessor. **The new station will be located in East Antarctica near the existing Maitri station** and is designed to accommodate approximately 90 scientists.
● **Maitri-II will be India’s fourth research centre in Antarctica.** The facility is due to come up by 2029. Maitri-2 will be developed and managed by the National Centre for Polar and Ocean Research (NCPOR).
● It is **planned to deploy automated instruments** onboard Maitri II which will keep recording data and relay it to mainland India, **even if the station remains unmanned for some period.** It will allow conduct of advanced studies in fields like climate change, glaciology, and biodiversity.
● **Maitri** was located along the Schirmacher Oasis, a 20-kilometre expanse of ice-free land in **Eastern** **Antarctica**.
○ India’s Antarctic journey began with Dakshin Gangotri, established in 1983. Currently, India’s research infrastructure consists of Maitri, which was built in 1989, and Bharati, which was completed in 2012. ○ Maitri-2 represents a crucial step in modernising India’s Antarctic research infrastructure. ○ The Indian Antarctic Programme has now credited to have built three permanent research base stations in Antarctica—named Dakshin Gangotri, Maitri, and Bharati. Maitri-II is next in line. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** National Centre for Polar and Ocean Research (NCPOR) has received in-principle approval for development of Maitri 2 station.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following best describes the
system of run-off election sometimes mentioned in the context of presidential democracies?

options.a:
It is an election held when no candidate
secures a majority in the first round of voting.
options.b:
It is an election conducted to fill a vacant
legislative seat before the normal term ends.
options.c:
It is an election held to choose a new leader
when the incumbent resigns or is removed from office.
options.d:
It is an election conducted when the
parliament decides to dissolve itself before completing its term.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In many countries, the president of the republic is directly elected through popular vote. Most such nations have an electoral system that provides for a double-round of voting.
● A run-off election is a second round of voting between the two candidates who got the most votes in the first round of voting. The run-off election is held if no candidate secures the required majority of votes in the first round.
● Voting mechanisms can vary by state; some states adopt a traditional , reflecting the runoff election meaning, where the top two candidates from the initial vote face off in a subsequent election. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Opposition leader Herminie won the presidential election in Seychelles, defeating incumbent leader Wavel Ramkalawan in a runoff vote.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Mass Extinctions and IUCN Red List
questions.questionText:
Recently, the International Union for Conservation
of Nature (IUCN) published an updated version of the Red List of Threatened Species. In this regard, consider the following statements
I. 	 Over half of all species assessed by the Red
List are now classified as ‘threatened with extinction’.
III. 	About 11.5% of bird species assessed are
globally threatened, and most species show declining populations.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has recently released an updated list of endangered species. The update was released at the IUCN World Conservation Congress 2025 in Abu Dhabi.
● The IUCN Red List now **includes "172,620 species of which 48,646 are threatened with extinction.**" **So, statement I is not correct.**
● This IUCN Red List update includes reassessments of 1,360 bird species. **About 11.5% of the 11,185** **species assessed are globally threatened.** Overall, 61% of bird species have declining populations. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● This Red List update also sees **six species moving to the Extinct category**, including the Christmas Island shrew and a species of cone snail, both of which have become extinct since the late 1980s; the slenderbilled curlew (Numenius tenuirostris), a migratory shorebird last recorded in Morocco in 1995; and Diospyros angulata, a species in the same genus as ebony trees, last recorded in the early 1850s.
● The update shows that the **hooded seal (Cystophora cristata) has declined from Vulnerable to** **Endangered,** while the bearded seal (Erignathus barbatus) and harp seal (Pagophilus groenlandicus) have moved from Least Concern to Near Threatened.
● **The Green Sea Turtle (Chelonia mydas) has improved in status from Endangered to Least Concern**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) World Conservation Congress 2025:**
● It is organised every four years, and **recently it took place in Abu Dhabi**, United Arab Emirates, from 9 to 15 October 2025.
● It enables IUCN Member organisations to democratically determine the most pressing issues in nature conservation.
● At the Congress, I**ndia unveiled the roadmap for the national-level five-year (2025-2030)** **assessment**. It aims to establish a nationally coordinated, participatory, and upgradable ‘Red Listing’ system that accurately reflects the conservation status of India’s biodiversity.
● A motion was adopted at the Congress to **recognise India’s first dugong conservation reserve** **in Tamil Nadu’s Palk Bay as a global model for marine biodiversity conservation**.
● **Vivek Menon,** the founder of the Wildlife Trust of India (WTI), has been named as the **new chair** **of the Species Survival Commission (SSC)**, making him the first Asian to hold the post.
● **Sonali Ghosh**, the Director of Kaziranga National Park and Tiger Reserve, has become the **first** **Indian to receive the prestigious WCPA-Kenton Miller Award** for innovation in national parks and protected area sustainability.
● The IUCN Congress has **unveiled the Abu Dhabi Call to Action,** outlining a 20-year strategic vision that sets out IUCN’s vision for “a just world that values and conserves nature”.
**Relevance:** The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has recently released an updated Red List of Threatened Species.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
Which of the following countries share land
border with Mongolia?
I. 	 Russia
II. 	 China
III. 	Kazakhstan
IV. 	 Kyrgyzstan
Select the correct answer using the code below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Mongolia is the world's second-largest landlocked country. **Mongolia shares its land borders with** **China and Russia. So, point I and II are correct.**
● Ulaanbaatar is the capital and most populous city of Mongolia. It is situated to the north of Tianjin, east of Bishkek**,** east of Baku and southeast of Kazan.
● Apart from Russia and China, the nation closest to Mongolia is Kazakhstan. Although the two nations do not share a common land boundary, they are only 23 miles apart at their closest points. **So, point III is** **not correct.**
● Mongolia does not share a border with Kyrgyzstan; they are separated by China. **So, point IV is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The President of Mongolia undertook a State Visit to India from 13 to 16 October 2025, marking the 70th anniversary of the establishment of diplomatic relations.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Surrogacy (Regulation) Act, 2021:
I. 	 It prohibits commercial surrogacy but permits
altruistic surrogacy
II. 	 The age cap will not apply to couples with
frozen embryos before the commencement of the Act.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**The Surrogacy Act 2021** defines surrogacy as a practice where a woman gives birth to a child for an intending couple with the intention to hand it over to them after the birth.
● The object of this Act is to **prevent the exploitation of women who may be lured into the arrangements** **of commercial surrogacy**. Under the provisions of the Act, **altruistic surrogacy is permitted,** whereby the surrogate mother is required to carry the child to help another individual or couple **without receiving** **the monetary benefit other than medical and insurance expenses. So, statement I is correct.**
● A **child born out of the surrogacy procedure shall be deemed to be the biological child of the** **intended couple** and shall be entitled to all the rights and privileges which are available to the natural child.
● The Act lays down eligibility conditions for couples intending to seek surrogacy. The law states that for intending couples, the woman must be between 23 and 50 years of age, and the man between 26 and 55 years. The Supreme Court recently ruled that the age limits under the Surrogacy (Regulation) Act, 2021, **do not apply to couples who had frozen their embryos and initiated the surrogacy process before** **the law came into force on January 25, 2022. So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Supreme Court recently said the retrospective age limits under the surrogacy law violate reproductive autonomy.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Tribe State
I. - Tikhir Nagaland
II. - Siddi Gujarat
III. Soliga Assam
IV. Kolam Kerala
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Tikhir** are an indigenous Naga tribe primarily from **Nagaland**, with some also living in Myanmar. They are an officially recognized Scheduled Tribe in India and speak the Naga Yimchungru language. **So,** **pair I is correctly matched.**
○ Historically, they were known for headhunting, and their traditional livelihoods are agriculture and hunting. ○ They observe the Tsonglaknyi festival from October 9-12, which involves the sanctification of shields and weapons.
● The Afro-Indian community, known as **Siddis**, with ancestral roots tracing back to East Africa, represents a unique diasporic tribe in India. The Afro-India community has been living in India since the 6th century, in the states of **Gujarat**, Karnataka, Goa, Maharashtra, etc. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Soliga Tribes** are a group of indigenous, forest-dwelling people found mostly in **Tamil Nadu and** **Karnataka**. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
○ The term “Soliga” literally translates to “children of bamboo”, which reflects the tribe’s relationship with nature and their belief that they too have emerged from it. ○ They reside in the peripheral forest areas near Biligiri Rangana Hills and Male Mahadeshwara Hills. ○ Soligas was the first tribal community living inside a tiger reserve and getting their forest rights recognised by a court in a judgment in 2011.
● **Kolam tribes**, also known as Kolamboli, Kulme and Kolmi, occupy a major portion of **Madhya Pradesh.** **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.**
○ These tribal groups are reckoned as scheduled tribes and apart from Madhya Pradesh they reside in some parts of Maharashtra and Andhra Pradesh. ○ They are listed as Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Group (PVTG). **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the President of India interacted with members of the Siddi community, a primitive tribal group with African roots, in Gujarat’s Junagadh district.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following countries has become
the first in the world to achieve 'triple elimination' of mother-to-child transmission of HIV, Syphilis and Hepatitis B?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In a landmark public health achievement, the **World Health Organisation has validated the Maldives** **for eliminating mother-to-child transmission of hepatitis B**, while maintaining its earlier validation for HIV and syphilis. This makes the **Maldives the first country in the world to achieve 'triple** **elimination'.**
● The Maldives has built an integrated and comprehensive approach to maternal and child health. More than 95 % of pregnant women receive antenatal care, with nearly universal testing for HIV, syphilis and hepatitis B.
● As a result, no babies were born with HIV or syphilis in 2022 and 2023, while a 2023 national survey confirmed zero hepatitis B among young children (first grade of school), surpassing elimination targets. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Maldives has become the first country in the world to achieve 'triple elimination'.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement-I:** Rhodamine B is widely used in textiles, paper and leather industries. **Statement-II:**
Rhodamine B makes the color durable and more resistant to fading over time.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the statements given above?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement-I.
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct,
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I.
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect.
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is
correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Rhodamine B** is a synthetic dye known for its bright pink hue, commonly **utilised in industries such as** **textiles, paper, and leather.** Its application extends to scientific research due to its fluorescent properties. **So, statement I is correct.**
● However, its **use in consumable products** is fraught with **health risks**. Studies have indicated that Rhodamine B can cause DNA damage, leading to mutations and potentially triggering cancerous growths. Animal research has demonstrated tumor development in organs like the liver and bladder following prolonged exposure to the dye.
● It is also a persistent environmental pollutant, often diluted in water to levels undetectable by standard techniques.
● It imparts a vivid, **fluorescent pink-to-red hue to materials**, making products stand out. The color is especially vibrant when it comes into contact with water.
● It is known for its stability and durability, meaning the color is resistant to fading over time. Its **water** **solubility does indeed make it suitable for uniform application** in these and other aqueous processes. **So, statement II is correct.** **So, both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct and Statement–II explains Statement–I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Scientists developed a powerful technique that uses the coffee-stain effect to identify harmful dyes such as Rhodamine B at concentrations as low as one part per trillion.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding the
Somnath temple:
I. 	 It was built in the traditional Chaulukya style
of architecture.
II. 	 It is situated at a Triveni sangam of three
rivers - Kapila, Hiran and Sarasvati.
III. 	Its most recent reconstruction was completed
in 1951 by Vallabhbhai Patel.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Somnath temple,** located in Prabhas Patan near Veraval in Saurashtra on the western coast of Gujarat, is believed to be the first among the twelve jyotirlinga shrines of **Shiva**. It is an important pilgrimage and tourist spot of Gujarat.
● Reconstructed several times in the past after repeated destruction by several Muslim invaders and Portuguese, the present temple was reconstructed in **Chaulukya style of Hindu temple architecture** and completed in May 1951. The reconstruction was completed by Vallabhbhai Patel. **So, statement I and** **III is correct.**
● The site of Somnath has been a pilgrimage site from ancient times on account of being a Triveni sangam (the confluence of three rivers — **Kapila, Hiran and Sarasvati**). **So, statement II is correct.**
● The ancient temple’s timeline can be traced from 649 BC but is believed to be older than that. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the President of India performed aarti and offered holy Ganga water to the deity at the Somnath temple.

questions.subject: Internal Security
questions.microTopic: Internal Security, Terrorism, Extremism, Cyber Security
questions.questionText:
The Doctrine of ‘SAMADHAN’ represents a
comprehensive framework of the Government of India to counter:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Left‐Wing Extremism (LWE), commonly known as Naxalism, has been one of the most persistent internal security challenges in India since the late 1960s.
● To effectively counter this complex threat, the Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) formulated a comprehensive doctrine called **‘SAMADHAN’** in 2017. The ‘SAMADHAN’ doctrine was introduced by the MHA under the leadership of the then Union Home Minister Shri Rajnath Singh.
● The acronym stands for the following components: ○ **S – Smart Leadership** ○ **A – Aggressive Strategy** ○ **M – Motivation and Training** ○ **A – Actionable Intelligence** ○ **D – Dashboard-Based Key Performance Indicators and Key Result Areas** ○ **H – Harnessing Technology** ○ **A – Action Plan for Each Theatre** ○ **N – No Access to Financing**
● Each of these components focuses on improving coordination among security forces, enhancing operational effectiveness, ensuring leadership accountability, and cutting off financial and logistical support to extremist groups. **Therefore, option (b) is the answer.**
**Relevance:** According to the Ministry of Home Affairs Annual Report (2023–24), incidents of Left-Wing Extremist violence have reduced by more than 75% since 2010, largely due to coordinated actions guided by SAMADHAN principles.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Which of the following factors are considered by
the Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP) while recommending the Minimum Support Price (MSP)?
I. 	 Cost of production
II. 	 Demand–supply situation of various crops
III. 	Inter-crop price parity
IV. 	 Terms of trade between the agriculture and
non-agriculture sectors
V. 	 Margin of at least 50% over the cost of
production
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

options.d: I, II, III, IV and V

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Minimum Support Price (MSP)** is announced by the Central Government at the beginning of each sowing season for certain crops on the recommendations of the Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP). The CACP suggests MSPs after a detailed analysis of various economic, agronomic, and market factors.
● The most fundamental factor in determining MSP is the **cost of production**. The CACP takes into account different types of cost calculations, namely A2, A2+FL, and C2, where C2 represents the most comprehensive cost, including imputed rent and interest on owned land and capital. **The MSP is ideally** **fixed to ensure that farmers recover at least the cost of cultivation, along with a reasonable profit** **margin**. **So, point I is correct**.
● While recommending MSP, the CACP examines the **overall demand and supply of different** **agricultural commodities, both in domestic and international markets.** This helps in maintaining price stability and avoiding excess production or shortages that could adversely affect the agricultural economy. The MSP thus balances farmer incentives with consumer interests. **So, point II is correct.**
● The CACP also considers **inter-crop price parity, which refers to maintaining a rational relationship** **between prices of different crops**. This ensures that farmers do not shift disproportionately toward one crop, thereby maintaining diversified cropping patterns and agro-ecological balance. **So, point III is** **correct.**
● The **terms of trade indicate the relative prices between what farmers sell (agricultural goods) and** **what they buy (industrial inputs, consumer goods, etc.).** If these terms become unfavorable to agriculture, farmers’ real incomes decline. Hence, CACP incorporates this factor to ensure parity between the agricultural and non-agricultural sectors, thereby protecting farmers’ purchasing power. **So, point IV** **is correct.**
● Following the recommendations of the Swaminathan Commission and subsequent government policy (Union Budget 2018–19), **the MSP is fixed to ensure a minimum 50% margin over the cost of** **production (A2+FL)**. This was institutionalized to guarantee profitability and reduce agrarian distress. **So, point V is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** In 2025, the Government announced a substantial increase in MSPs for Kharif crops like paddy, tur, and maize to ensure better returns to farmers..

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.microTopic: Allied Sectors (Animal Husbandry, Horticulture, Sericulture, Apiculture, Aquaculture)
questions.questionText:
The Sustainable Aquaculture in Mangrove
Ecosystems (SAIME) initiative, recently seen the news, is an initiative by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Mangrove ecosystems are vital coastal habitats that serve as natural buffers against cyclones, tidal surges, and coastal erosion while supporting a rich diversity of flora and fauna. They also provide livelihood opportunities to coastal communities through fisheries, aquaculture, and the collection of forest produce.
● **The SAIME project was conceptualised and implemented by the Nature Environment and Wildlife** **Society (NEWS)**, a Kolkata-based environmental NGO. The initiative integrates sustainable aquaculture with mangrove restoration, encouraging local communities to undertake eco-friendly aquaculture practices such as low-input fish and crab farming within or adjacent to mangrove habitats. This approach not only restores degraded mangrove areas but also provides sustainable livelihoods, reducing pressure on natural resources.
● The SAIME project **has been supported by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP)** **and the Global Environment Facility – Small Grants Programme (GEF-SGP)**. The initiative primarily operates in the Indian Sundarbans region of West Bengal, not along the Andhra Pradesh coast. Its geographical and institutional context is thus specific to eastern India, focusing on mangrovedependent communities in that region. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India strengthens its commitment to coastal ecosystem restoration under international frameworks like the UN Decade on Ecosystem Restoration (2021–2030).

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
With reference to green crackers  in India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 They were developed by the Council of
Scientific & Industrial Research – National Environmental Engineering Research Institute.
II. 	 Their manufacturing and sale licenses are
granted by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The widespread use of crackers has raised serious environmental and health concerns due to the emission of particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), sulphur oxides, and nitrogen oxides, all of which **contribute to severe air** **pollution.**
● **The green crackers were developed by CSIR-NEERI in collaboration with other CSIR laboratories** **such as the Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CECRI) and the National Chemical** **Laboratory (NCL).** The primary objective was to reduce harmful emissions by modifying the chemical composition of traditional fireworks. In green crackers, barium nitrate (a major source of heavy metal pollution and toxic emissions) has been eliminated or substantially reduced and replaced with less harmful oxidisers such as potassium nitrate, strontium nitrate, or other nitrogen-rich compounds. **So, statement I** **is correct.**
● **To ensure authenticity and prevent misuse of the “green cracker” label, CSIR-NEERI introduced** **a QR code-based certification system**. The Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation (PESO), under the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), is responsible for granting manufacturing and sale licenses only to those firecrackers that comply with CSIR-NEERI’s approved formulations and emission standards.  **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Supreme Court of India recently reiterated that only green crackers certified by CSIR-NEERI and licensed by PESO may be manufactured, sold, or used in India, especially in areas suffering from high air pollution, such as Delhi–NCR.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Pradhan Mantri Formalisation
of Micro Food Processing Enterprises (PM‑FME) Scheme, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It provides assistance to entrepreneurs only
to upgrade existing units.
II. 	 It provides support only to collective entities
such as Farmer Producer Organisations and Self-Help Groups.
III. 	It aims to promote one unique product per
district that reflects the district’s comparative advantage.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**The Pradhan Mantri Formalisation of Micro Food Processing Enterprises** **Scheme** was launched on **29** **June** **2020**. It is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme that focuses on the growth and formalisation of micro food units across the country.
● The scheme is part of the **Atmanirbhar Bharat Abhiyaan** and supports the **Vocal for Local vision** in the food processing sector. **It provides financial, technical and business assistance to entrepreneurs** **for setting up new units or upgrading existing ones.** The scheme runs from **2020-21 to 2025-26** with an outlay of **Rs. 10,000 crores**. Its goal is to make micro enterprises more competitive, bring them into the organised sector and unlock new opportunities for growth. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The program has 4 broad components addressing the needs of the sector:
● **Support for Individual Units** ○ Credit-linked capital subsidy of **35%** of the project cost. ○ Maximum ceiling of **₹10 lakh per unit**. ○ Minimum **10%** beneficiary contribution, balance through bank loan. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● **Support for Farmer-Producer Organisations (FPOs) and Producer Co-operatives** ○ Grant support at **35%** with credit linkage. ○ Training and capacity building provided. ○ Maximum funding as prescribed under scheme norms.
● **Support for Self-Help Groups (SHGs)** ○ **₹40,000** per SHG member for working capital and small tools. ○ Priority for SHGs working on ODOP (One District One Approach) products. ○ Seed capital given at the federation level and disbursed as a repayable loan to members.
● **The One District One Product approach is a key feature of the PM-FME Scheme**. It aims to identify and promote one unique product per district that reflects the district’s comparative advantage, raw material availability, and traditional food identity—such as mango in Uttar Pradesh, pickle in Andhra Pradesh, or turmeric in Odisha. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** As of **June 2025**, a total of **1,44,517** loans of amount **Rs. 11,501.79 crore** has been sanctioned to individual micro food processing units and groups for credit linked subsidy across the country.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** As the Southwest Monsoon withdraws, Tamil Nadu, coastal Andhra Pradesh, and parts of Kerala experience rainfall.
**Statement II:** During the post-monsoon period, there is formation of a high-pressure area over the Tibetan–Siberian region.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Northeast Monsoon, also known as the retreating monsoon, is the secondary rainfall season for India, occurring mainly from October to December. While the Southwest Monsoon (June–September) covers most of India, the Northeast Monsoon brings rain primarily to the southeastern coast, including Tamil Nadu, coastal Andhra Pradesh, Kerala, and parts of Karnataka.
● As the Southwest Monsoon withdraws, the ITCZ shifts southward, following the apparent movement of the Sun. This shift alters the large-scale circulation pattern over the Indian subcontinent. **The** **northeasterly winds that originate over the continental high-pressure area cross the Bay of Bengal,** **where they pick up moisture. When these moist winds strike the eastern slopes of the Western Ghats** **and the Coromandel Coast**, they produce rainfall over Tamil Nadu, coastal Andhra Pradesh, and parts of Kerala. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **During the post-monsoon period**, the northern landmass of India and the adjoining Tibetan Plateau cooled rapidly due to the withdrawal of solar heating. This leads to the formation of a **high-pressure area** **over the Tibetan–Siberian region.** The air over this region becomes cold and dense, leading to outward movement of air from the high-pressure area towards the low-pressure zones over the Indian Ocean and the Bay of Bengal. This reversal of pressure gradient causes the prevailing winds to blow from the northeast to the southwest, thereby marking the **onset of the Northeast Monsoon**. **So, statement II is** **correct.** **So, both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct and Statement–II is the correct explanation for** **Statement–I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has emphasised that variability in the timing and intensity of the Northeast Monsoon is increasingly linked to climate change, El Niño–Southern Oscillation (ENSO) patterns, and warming of the Indian Ocean.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
With reference to India’s relations with Afghanistan, consider the following statements:
I. 	 India does not share a direct land border with
Afghanistan.
II. 	 The Zaranj–Delaram Highway and the Salma
Dam are infrastructure projects built by India in Afghanistan.
III. 	India has created an alternative trade and
transit route for Afghanistan through the Chabahar Port in Iran.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
India and Afghanistan share a historically deep relationship rooted in cultural, economic, and civilizational ties dating back to the ancient Silk Route. In modern times, India has played a significant role in Afghanistan’s reconstruction, humanitarian assistance, and capacity building, especially after 2001. India’s policy towards Afghanistan has aimed to promote stability, democracy, and development, without any military involvement.
● As per the official records of the Government of India and maps released by the Survey of India, **India’s** **boundary with Afghanistan is about 106 km long, running through the Wakhan Corridor** — a narrow strip of land in northeastern Afghanistan. This corridor separates Tajikistan from Pakistanoccupied Gilgit-Baltistan and meets the easternmost point of Ladakh (UT of India). **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● India has been a major development partner of Afghanistan, committing billions of USD in assistance for multiple projects. Among them, two flagship projects stand out: The **Zaranj–Delaram Highway,**
constructed by India s Border Roads Organisation (BRO), provides Afghanistan with a strategic trade route to Iran’s Chabahar Port, bypassing Pakistan and **the Salma Dam or Afghan–India Friendship** **Dam in Herat province**, built with Indian funding, provides hydropower and irrigation to thousands of Afghan farmers. These projects symbolise India’s developmental approach and long-term commitment to Afghanistan’s reconstruction. **So, statement II is correct.**
● India’s approach to Afghanistan has emphasised soft power and regional connectivity. **Through the** **Chabahar Port in Iran, India has sought to create an alternative trade and transit route for** **Afghanistan** that circumvents Pakistan. Additionally, India has contributed to education, healthcare, skill development, and the training of Afghan officials and students. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently the Acting Foreign Minister of Afghanistan, Amir Khan Muttaqi, visited.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
MERCOSUR:
I. 	 It is a trading bloc in Latin America
comprising Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay and Paraguay.
II. 	 India has signed a Free Trade Agreement
with the MERCOSUR to eliminate tariffs on most goods and services.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **MERCOSUR** is a trading bloc in Latin America comprising **Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay and Paraguay**. MERCOSUR was formed in 1991 with the objective of facilitating the free movement of goods, services, capital and people among the four member countries. It is the third largest integrated market after the European Union (EU), North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA). **So, statement I is correct.**
● A Framework Agreement had been signed between India and MERCOSUR on 2003. As a follow up to the Framework Agreement, a **Preferential Trade Agreement (PTA)** was signed in New Delhi on January 25, 2004. The aim of this Preferential Trade Agreement is to expand and strengthen the existing relations between MERCOSUR and India and promote the expansion of trade by granting reciprocal fixed tariff preferences with the ultimate objective of creating a free trade area between the parties. **So, statement II** **is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India and the MERCOSUR Member States have decided to  deepen their existing Preferential Trade Agreement, with a view to promoting trade and investment between the Parties.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Leprosy in India:
I. 	 It is one of the Neglected Tropical Diseases
(NTDs).
II. 	 India eliminated leprosy as a public health
problem.
III. 	It is treated through the Multi-Drug Therapy
regimen.
How many of the above statements are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Leprosy**, also known as Hansen’s disease, is a chronic infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium leprae, which primarily affects the skin, peripheral nerves, and mucous membranes. Despite being curable, leprosy remains a public health concern in many developing countries due to its association with social stigma and disability.
● **The World Health Organisation includes leprosy in its list of Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs)** — a group of conditions that primarily affect poor and marginalised populations in tropical and subtropical regions. These diseases are termed “neglected” because they receive less attention and fewer resources compared to other major diseases like HIV, malaria, or tuberculosis. The classification aims to ensure focused global action for their elimination. Leprosy’s inclusion highlights the need for sustained awareness, early detection, and disability prevention efforts. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The World Health Organisation defines **“elimination of leprosy as a public health problem”** as reaching a prevalence rate of **less than 1 case per 10,000 population**. **India officially achieved this goal at the** **national level in December 2005.** However, it is important to note that “elimination” does not mean complete eradication — new cases still occur annually in several states and districts. According to the National Leprosy Eradication Programme (NLEP), India still contributes to over half of the global leprosy cases, indicating the need for continued vigilance. **So, statement II is correct.**
● In the early 1980s, the **World Health Organization (WHO) recommended Multi-Drug Therapy** **(MDT)** as a game-changer in leprosy treatment. Responding to this, India restructured NLCP into the
National Leprosy Eradication Programme(NLEP) in 1983, shifting the focus from control to elimination. MDT was introduced nationwide, and the programme adopted strategies to reduce transmission by detecting and treating all cases promptly. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the answer.**
**Relevance:** India has renewed efforts toward “zero leprosy transmission by 2030” under the Global Leprosy Strategy 2021–2030 of the WHO.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements about the Food
and Agriculture Organisation:
I. - It is a specialised agency of the United Nations that leads international efforts to defeat hunger.
II. 	 It provides food aid to improve the nutrition of
vulnerable people in times of crisis.
III. 	 It publishes the State of Food Security and
Nutrition in the World report.
Which of the above statements are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) is a specialised agency of the United Nations that** **leads international efforts to defeat hunger.** Its goal is to achieve food security for all and make sure that people have regular access to enough high-quality food to lead active, healthy lives. **So, statement I** **is not correct.**
● FAO is a knowledge organization. It is the lead UN agency for technical expertise in food security, agriculture, forestry, fisheries and rural development, as well as in the sustainable management of natural resources essential over the long term.
○ **The World Food Programme** is a humanitarian organization. It provides food aid and logistical operations to save lives in emergency situations and to improve the nutrition and quality of life of the most vulnerable people in times of crisis. **So, statement II is not correct.** ○ **International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD**)  is a multilateral financial institution. It funds rural development projects that will improve the nutritional level and living conditions of the poorest populations in developing countries. ○ The organizations have complementary mandates that allow for joint programming and collaboration on their shared goal of ensuring the fundamental right of all people to be free from hunger.
● FAO maintains a Publications Catalogue, and it has a “flagship publications” series including **The State** **of Food Security and Nutrition in the World,** The State of Food and Agriculture, The State of the World’s Forests, and The State of Agricultural Commodity Markets. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** In 2025, FAO celebrated its 80-year anniversary and reaffirmed its commitment to ending hunger and transforming agrifood systems.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part I: The Union and its territory
questions.microTopic: Union, States and their Territory, UTs
questions.questionText:
Which of the following Indian States or Union
Territories have more than one capital?
I. - Jammu and Kashmir
II. 	 Himachal Pradesh
III. 	 Maharashtra
IV. 	 Arunachal Pradesh
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Some Indian States and Union Territories maintain more than one capital to balance administrative convenience, geography, and regional representation.
● **Jammu and Kashmir:** Has two capitals under the traditional Darbar Move, **Srinagar (summer capital)** **and Jammu (winter capital)**. This 150-year-old practice, started by Maharaja Gulab Singh in 1872, was halted in 2021 but is being revived in 2025 after approval by CM Omar Abdullah and the Lieutenant Governor. **So, point I is correct.**
● The capital of **Himachal Pradesh** in summer is **Shimla, and in winter its capital is Dharamshala.** Two capitals have been decided because of the extreme cold during winter. Because many major routes get closed when it snows here. This is the reason why Himachal Pradesh has two capitals. **So, point II is** **correct.**
● While **Mumbai** is the official capital of **Maharashtra**, **Nagpur** is considered its "second capital" and serves as the location for the State's winter session of the legislature, following the Nagpur Pact of 1953.
This practice aims to ensure development and representation for the Vidarbha region, which is geographically distant from Mumbai. The budget and monsoon sessions are held in Mumbai. **So, point** **III is not correct.**
● **Arunachal Pradesh has only one capital,** Itanagar, not multiple ones. **So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Darbar Move, the over a century-and-a-half-old practice that sees shifting of the Civil Secretariat and other government offices from Jammu to Srinagar in summer, and vice versa in winter is all set to make a comeback after a gap of four years.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Major Species (Keystone, Indicator, Invasive, Endemic, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the species of Kurunji, consider
the following statements:
I. - It is known as the flower which makes the Neelgiri hills blue.
II. 	 It is considered an indicator of climate change.
III. 	 It grows primarily in the Eravikulam National
Park.
IV. 	 It is included under Schedule V of the Wildlife
(Protection) Act, 1972
Select the correct answer using the code below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Kurniji is the famous flower which makes the** **Neelgiri** **hills blue**. Kurinji is a bright blue bell-shaped flower found on the hill slopes of the Western Ghats at an altitude between 6000 to 7000 feet. The unique feature of this flower is that it blooms once in 12 years! **So, statement I is correct.**
● The kurinji flower is considered an **indicator of climate change** because its unique blooming cycle is sensitive to environmental changes like rising temperatures and rainfall patterns. A healthy, mass flowering indicates thriving grasslands, while changes in the timing or health of the bloom can signal a disruption in the ecosystem, making it a key species for studying the effects of climate change in the Western Ghats. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Kurinji (specifically the** **Strobilanthes kunthiana** **species) grows primarily in the Eravikulam** **National Park and the Kurinjimala Sanctuary,** both located in the Western Ghats mountain range in Kerala. **So, statement III is correct.**
● T**he Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF) has listed Neelakurinji** **(Strobilanthes kunthiana) under Schedule III of the Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972**, including it on the list of protected plants. Neelakurinji has been included in the list after the Center increased the earlier protected list of six plant species to 19. Those who uproot or destroy the plant will invite a fine of ₹25,000 and three years’ imprisonment. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Kurinji flower has bloomed in Tamil Nadu's Gudalur Reserve Forest after eight years, indicating both a successful recovery of the grassland ecosystem due to conservation efforts and the influence of climate change.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Money (Evolution, Types, Digital Currency, Crypto, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to
the Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC):
I. - It is the legal tender issued in a digital form.
II. 	 It is not permitted in cross-border transactions.
III. 	 It is backed by gold reserves.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**:
● **According to the RBI, “CBDC is the legal tender** issued by a central bank in a digital form. It is the **same as a fiat currency** and is exchangeable one-to-one **with the fiat currency.** Only its form is different.” The digital fiat currency or CBDC can be **transacted using wallets backed by blockchain**. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ In December 2022, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) launched India’s CBDC, known as the digital rupee or e-rupee, on a pilot basis for retail users. Currently, RBI is conducting pilot projects of two types of CBDCs – retail and wholesale. ○ **CBDCs are different from decentralised virtual currencies** and **crypto assets**, which are **not** **issued by the state and lack the ‘legal tender’ status.**
● **CBDCs** can be tokenised and have the advantages of singleness of money, the integrity of money. **CBDCs** **enable the user to conduct both domestic and cross-border transactions** which do not require a third party or a bank. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Stablecoins are** **blockchain-based digital assets** designed to maintain a consistent value over time. **They** **are backed by reserves such as fiat currencies, commodities, or other crypto assets**.
○ A **CBDC is not backed by other assets**; it is the sovereign currency itself. CBDCs are direct liabilities of the central bank, just as paper cash is. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Types of Stablecoins and its Global status** **There are two categories of stablecoins**: fully reserved stablecoins and algorithmic stablecoins.
● **Fully reserved stablecoins**: These types of stablecoins are backed one-to-one by high-quality, liquid assets like fiat currency or short-term government securities held in the reserve. **It offers** **a stable price** as each issued coin is supported by an underlying asset.
● **Algorithmic stablecoins:** “These maintain their peg through smart contracts that respond to supply and demand imbalances by minting or burning tokens.
○ If an algorithmic stablecoin trades above its peg, the protocol mints additional tokens to reduce its price. Conversely, if it trades below its peg, the protocol burns tokens to increase its price.”
● In June, the US Senate passed the Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for US Stablecoins Act (GENIUS Act).
● In the same month, South Korea’s National Assembly passed the Digital Asset Basic Act to legalize South Korean Won (KRW) denominated stablecoins.
● In May, Hong Kong ‘s Legislative Council passed a stablecoin legislation to establish a licensing regime for local ‘fiat-referenced stablecoins’ issuers.
**Relevance:** RBI Governor urges other central banks to promote CBDCs over stablecoins.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Poverty Line in India, which
of the following statements are correct?
I. 	 The Rangarajan Committee fixed the poverty
line based on monthly per capita expenditure.
II. 	 Odisha and Bihar have shown the greatest
reduction in poverty among 20 major states over the past decade.
III. 	The Household Consumption Expenditure
Survey (HCES) provides the key data used for calculating and updating poverty lines in India.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The World Bank has announced a major revision to global poverty estimates, raising the International Poverty Line (IPL) from $2.15/day (2017 PPP) to $3.00/day (2021 PPP). While the change led to a global increase in the count of extreme poverty by 125 million, India emerged as a statistical outlier in a positive direction. India’s latest Household Consumption Expenditure Survey (HCES) adopted the Modified Mixed Recall Period (MMRP) method, replacing the outdated Uniform Reference Period (URP). This shift:
● Used shorter recall periods for frequently purchased items
● Captured more realistic estimates of actual consumption
● **The C. Rangarajan Committee (2014) revised the poverty line to ₹972 (rural) and ₹1,407 (urban)** **per capita per month, translating to about ₹32 and ₹47 per day, respectively.** This raised the estimated poverty ratio to 29.5% of India’s population. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **A 2025 RBI study updating the Rangarajan poverty line across 20 major states found that Odisha** **and Bihar made the largest gains in poverty reduction**, though both started from a low base. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● **The Household Consumption Expenditure Survey (HCES) of the National Sample Survey Office** **(NSSO) is the primary data source for estimating consumption and poverty** across rural and urban areas. **So, statement III is correct.**
● **The Planning Commission was replaced by NITI Aayog in 2015, and no official poverty line has** **been revised since 2014; hence, it is not updated every five years**. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● **Purpose of the Rangarajan Method:** To include better nutritional, health, and education norms than the earlier Tendulkar formula.
● **Poverty Ratio (2011–12):** 29.5% (Rangarajan) vs. 21.9% (Tendulkar).
● **HCES Significance (2022–23):** Used for revising poverty lines and CPI weights, capturing household expenditure patterns post-pandemic.
● **State Performers:** Odisha, Bihar, and Chhattisgarh have shown sharp reductions; Tamil Nadu and Kerala maintain low poverty levels.
**Relevance:** In a recent study, economists from the RBI updated the Rangarajan poverty line for 20 major states. The result showed Odisha and Bihar have made the biggest improvement over the last decade or so, albeit over a low base.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.microTopic: Current Affairs Focus (Summits, Reports, Conflicts, Visits, etc.)
questions.questionText:
A behavioural condition is being increasingly
observed among children raised in small, urban nuclear families. The child is overindulged by parents and grandparents, given everything they demand without limits or responsibility. This excessive pampering inhibits emotional growth, makes them unable to handle failure or denial, and often leads to irritability, overconfidence, and difficulty adjusting socially as they grow older.
Which of the following syndromes is described here?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Six-Pocket Syndrome** refers to a **pattern of overpampering a child by six adults, two parents** **and four grandparents who provide constant material and emotional indulgence**.
● Originating in China during the one-child policy era, it now describes modern Indian family dynamics where children receive unconditional attention and resources but limited discipline or resilience training.
● Psychiatrists note that such children often develop entitlement, emotional instability, and poor coping mechanisms when faced with real-world rejection or failure.
○ Root cause: Overindulgence due to guilt or limited parental time. ○ Consequences: Emotional immaturity, poor social adaptability, and dependence. ○ Correction model: Linking rewards to responsibilities, setting boundaries, and fostering accountability. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A young contestant’s nonchalant attitude and over-confidence on Kaun Banega Crorepati has sparked a huge online debate on parenting and whether we are mollycoddling a generation into demanding the right of way.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct?
I. 	 Electric vehicles (EV) are exempted from
paying the road tax.
II. 	 The Government of India has set a target of
50% EV penetration by 2030.
III. 	PM-E-DRIVE offers targeted incentives
to strengthen domestic manufacturing capabilities.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Despite high subsidies, India has achieved very low EV adoption, making up just two per cent of the vehicle market, which is the lowest when compared to other countries.
● **India provides some of the highest EV subsidies in the world,** with total benefits, including reduced GST, **road tax exemptions,** and PLI-linked support, covering around 46% of the price of models like the
Tata Nexon EV. Comparable figures are much lower in countries such as China (10%), Germany (20%), and the US and Japan (26%). **So, statement I is correct.**
● **The Government of India has set an ambitious target of 30% EV penetration by 2030.** The intent is to have 30% of all cars, 70% of all commercial vehicles and 80% of all two-wheelers sold from 2030 to be electric vehicles. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **The Government has introduced the PM-Electric Drive Vehicle Incentive and Ecosystem (PM-E-** **DRIVE) scheme**, launched on 13 March 2024. The scheme is aimed specifically at catalysing the adoption of electric vehicles, including segments with high impact potential such as e-buses and e-trucks. PM-EDRIVE is designed to address remaining barriers by offering **targeted incentives, strengthening** **domestic manufacturing capabilities**, and promoting localized innovation across the electric mobility value chain. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● Subsidy Phasing: 30% released on tender award, 40% after installation, and the remaining after commercial operation of charging stations.
● Global Context: Despite generous incentives, India’s EV penetration remains only 2% of total vehicle sales — among the lowest globally.
● WTO Angle (2025): China has lodged a WTO complaint claiming India’s EV subsidies distort trade and provide an “unfair competitive advantage” to domestic producers.
**Relevance:** China recently filed a complaint with the World Trade Organization (WTO) over India's electric vehicle (EV) and battery subsidies, claiming they give an "unfair competitive advantage" to the domestic manufacturers.

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.microTopic: Current Affairs Focus (Summits, Reports, Conflicts, Visits, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which key outcome document was adopted at the
first ARISE Cities Forum 2025 in New Delhi to advance multilevel urban climate action across the Global South?

options.c:
Delhi Declaration on Local Action for Global
Climate Goals

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **first ARISE Cities Forum 2025** (October 8–9, 2025), co-hosted by ICLEI South Asia and the National Institute of Urban Affairs (NIUA), brought together delegates from 60 cities and 25 countries.
● The forum adopted the **Delhi Declaration on Local Action for Global Climate Goals**, a landmark statement uniting Global South cities to advance urban resilience, equity, climate finance, and multilevel governance ahead of COP30 in Belém. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** COP30, the Delhi Declaration on Local Action for Global Climate Goals emerged as the key outcome of the first ARISE Cities Forum 2025, held in New Delhi.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Wildlife Conservation (Tiger, Elephant, Lion, Rhino, Crocodile, Vulture, Tortoise, Dolphin, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct
about the Blackbuck?
I. 	 It was reintroduced in the Barnawapara
Wildlife Sanctuary in Chhattisgarh after being locally extinct for nearly 50 years.
II. 	 It gets the highest level of legal protection
under the Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Black buck is a species of medium-sized antelope native to India and Nepal. It is known for its beautiful spiraling horns. Only the male blackbucks have these horns. They can grow quite long, sometimes over 20 inches.
● The blackbuck, a medium-sized antelope once native to Chhattisgarh, was declared locally extinct in 2017 after disappearing for five decades due to poaching, habitat loss, and encroachment.
● Under a five-year revival plan (2021–2026), 77 blackbucks were translocated, 50 from the National Zoological Park, Delhi, and 27 from the Kanan Pendari Zoo, Bilaspur, leading to a thriving population of
around 190 individuals in **Barnawapara Wildlife Sanctuary**, Balodabazar district. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **The blackbuck is protected under Schedule I of the Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972, offering it the** **highest level of legal protection.** It is currently listed as Least Concern (LC) on the IUCN Red List, though populations remain locally threatened. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
**Blackbuck**
● Scientific name: Antilope cervicapra
● Habitat: Open grasslands and semi-arid plains of India and Nepal.
● Conservation model: Enclosures, habitat restoration (Rampur grasslands), and antipoaching measures were key to the Barnawapara success.
● It is widespread in the states of Rajasthan, Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Odisha, and other areas throughout peninsular India.
● It has been declared as the state animal by the governments of Punjab, Haryana, and Andhra Pradesh.
**Relevance:** Over the last five years, the Chhattisgarh government has successfully reintroduced the blackbuck back into the state’s forests through its five-year reintroduction plan.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Biodiversity (IUCN, WWF, CBD, Cartagena, Nagoya, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following bodies/treaties is
primarily responsible for the management and oversight of the Species Survival Commission (SSC)?

options.a:
Convention on International Trade in
Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora
options.c:
International Union for Conservation of
Nature

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● The **Species Survival Commission (SSC)** is **one of the seven expert commissions of the** International Union for Conservation of Nature (**IUCN).** It is a science-based network of more than 10,500 volunteer experts from almost every country of the world. **SSC’s major role is to provide information to IUCN** **on biodiversity conservation**, the inherent value of species, their role in ecosystem health and functioning, the provision of ecosystem services, and their support to human livelihoods. This information is fed into the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species. **So, option (c) is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● Other **Expert commission of IUCN**: ○ **World Commission on Protected Areas (WCPA):** Generates knowledge, policy, and guidance to improve the planning and management of protected areas globally (e.g., national parks, wildlife sanctuaries). ○ **Commission on Environmental, Economic and Social Policy (CEESP):** Provides expertise and policy advice on integrating environmental, economic, and social factors for the sustainable management of natural resources. ○ **Commission on Ecosystem Management (CEM):** Provides guidance on integrated ecosystem management to conserve biodiversity and provide naturebased solutions. ○ **Commission on Education and Communication (CEC):** Promotes the use of communication and education strategies to empower people to act for biodiversity conservation and sustainable development. ○ **IUCN Academy of Environmental Law (IUCN AEL):** It is the network of environmental law experts that contributes directly to the IUCN's legal and governance work, providing a global network of legal expertise.
○ **Climate Action Commission**: It is the newest Commission, previously called the Climate Crisis Commission, established at the World Conservation Congress in Marseille in 2022.
■ The Commission was renamed as the Climate Action Commission (CAC) at the 2025 IUCN World Conservation Congress in Abu Dhabi ■ The mission of CAC will be to promote appropriate, effective, just, and nature-positive solutions to the climate crisis that are based on the best available scientific evidence from all reputable international bodies and from indigenous knowledge systems.
**Relevance:** Indian conservationist Vivek Menon becomes 1st Asian Chair of IUCN Species Survival Commission.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Global Multidimensional
Poverty Index (MPI), 2025, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It, for the first time, mapped poor populations’
exposure to heat, drought, floods, and air pollution.
II. 	 South Asia has the highest number of
multidimensionally poor people exposed to one or more climate hazards.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**The Global Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI) released by UNDP-OPHI,**  is an international measure of acute multidimensional poverty covering over 100 developing countries. It complements traditional monetary poverty measures by capturing the acute deprivations in health, education, and living standards that a person faces simultaneously.
● The 2025 Global Multidimensional Poverty Index, jointly released by UNDP and OPHI, **incorporated** **for the first time a mapping of poor populations’ exposure to four climate risks, high heat, drought,** **floods, and air pollution** highlighting the overlap between poverty and climate vulnerability. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The report identifies **South Asia as the most climate-exposed region**, where 99.1% of multidimensionally poor people live in areas facing at least one climate hazard, and 59% (around 226 million) are exposed to three or more overlapping hazards. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● Global context: 1.1 billion people are multidimensionally poor; 887 million live in climate-hazard zones.
● Regional figures: South Asia (380 million poor) and Sub-Saharan Africa (344 million) remain the global poverty–climate hotspots.
● India’s progress: The proportion of multidimensionally poor fell from 55.1% (2005–06) to 16.4% (2019–21), with 414 million people exiting poverty.
● Key message: Poverty and climate risks are now interlinked global challenges, demanding integrated policy responses to prevent reversal of poverty reduction gains.
**Relevance:** Climate change will drive more to poverty, and make poverty persistent, says the latest Multidimensional Poverty Index.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements about the
Prime Minister’s Schools for Rising India
(PM-SHRI) scheme:
I. 	 These schools will be showcased as
exemplary schools for the implementation of the National Education Policy, 2020.
II. 	 The school infrastructure during non-teaching
hours would be used as Common Service Centers.
III. 	A maximum of two schools will be selected
from every block or urban local body.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **PM SHRI Schools (PM Schools for Rising India)** was announced by Prime Minister Narendra Modi on National Teachers’ Day in 2022. The scheme seeks to develop over 14,500 existing schools, including Kendriya Vidyalayas (KVs) and Jawahar Navodaya Vidyalayas (JNVs), across the country as model institutions that showcase the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020. Thus, it aims to provide high-quality education in an equitable, inclusive and joyful school environment.
● **These schools will help showcase the implementation of the National Education Policy 2020 and** **emerge as exemplary schools.** They will provide leadership in their respective regions in providing high quality education in an equitable, inclusive and joyful school environment that takes care of the diverse background, multilingual needs, and different academic abilities of children and makes them active participants in their learning process. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The unutilized capacity of school infrastructure would be used to promote social, intellectual, and volunteer activities for the community and to promote social cohesion during non-teaching / schooling hours and **may be used as a “Samajik Chetna Kendra”. So, statement II is not correct.**
● **The selection is done in three stages**. **First**, States or UTs sign a memorandum of understanding (MoU) with the Union government agreeing to implement the provisions of NEP 2020 in “entirety”. **Then,** **schools that meet the minimum benchmark (based on UDISE+ data) are shortlisted**.
o **In the third stage**, the shortlisted schools compete to fulfil certain criteria based on the challenge formula. Their claims are verified by States or UTs through physical inspection and a list of schools is recommended to the Union Ministry of Education. o **A maximum of two schools — one elementary and one secondary/senior secondary — are** **selected from every block or urban local body. So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Kerala signed up for the Union government’s flagship PM-SHRI education scheme.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
questions.questionText:
With reference to municipal finances in India,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Constitution of India empowers the
Parliament to specify by law matters relating to imposition of taxes by municipalities.
II. 	 After the introduction of the Goods and
Services Tax (GST), municipalities lost their taxation power on Entry Tax.
III. 	The audit of the accounts of the municipalities
is done as per the provisions of the State law.
IV. 	 Municipal bonds cannot be pledged as a
security for repo and reverse repo transactions.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Constitution (74th Amendment) Act, 1992 has introduced a new Part IXA** in the Constitution, which deals with Municipalities in an article 243P to 243ZG.  This amendment, **also known as Nagarpalika** **Act**, came into force on 1st June 1993.
o It has given constitutional status to the municipalities and brought them under the justifiable part of the constitution. States were put under constitutional obligation to adopt municipalities as per system enshrined in the constitution.
● **Article 243X of the Constitution of India empowers the Legislature of a State** (not Parliament), to specify by law matters relating to the imposition of taxes, duties, tolls, Grant-in-aid that would be given to the Municipalities from the State and fees to be collected by the Municipalities. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● The **introduction of the GST subsumed indirect taxes**, including Entry Tax (Octroi), which was a significant source of revenue for many municipalities. While they retain property tax, the loss of these major taxes was a key impact of GST. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **As per article 243Z**, the maintenance of the **accounts of the municipalities** and other **audits shall be** **done in accordance with the provisions in the State law**. The State Legislatures will be free to make appropriate provisions in this regard depending upon the local needs and institutional framework available for this purpose. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The **Finance Ministry has permitted in October 2025,** using **municipal bonds as a security for repo** **and reverse repo transactions**, paving the way for municipal bodies to raise funds for infrastructure projects. By making these bonds acceptable collateral for short-term borrowing, the government has opened the door for banks, mutual funds, insurers, and companies to invest in them, creating a new asset class. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Constitutional status of Municipalities**
● **Article 243Q provides for the establishment of 3 kinds of Municipalities of every state.** o **Nagar Panchayat**: A Nagar Panchayat is for those areas which are transitional areas i.e. transiting from Rural Area to Urban areas. o **Municipal Council:** for smaller urban area o **Municipal Corporation**: for Larger urban Areas.
● **All the members of a Municipality are to be directly elected** by the people of the Municipal area and for the purpose of making the electorate; the municipal area will be divided into territorial constituencies known as Wards.
o The manner of election of Chairpersons of municipalities has been left to be specified by the State Legislature (Article 243R).
● **Duration of the municipality has been fixed at 5 years** from the date appointed for its first meeting. Elections to constitute a municipality are required to be completed before the expiration of the duration of the municipality.
● **Article 243ZB makes provisions for applications of these provisions to the Union** **Territories.**
o This article says that the provisions of Municipalities shall be applicable to the UTs in the same way as in case of the states but the President by a public notification may make any modifications in the applications of any part.
**Relevance**: Recently the Finance Ministry has permitted using municipal bonds as a security for repo and reverse repo transactions.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.questionText:
With reference to the United Nations (UN), which
of the statements given below are correct?
I. 	 It was created under the Atlantic Charter
which aimed to provide collective security.
II. 	 Its principal judicial organ is the International
Court of Justice.
III. 	The "UN75 Declaration" sets out 12
commitments to guide global cooperation over the coming decade.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **United Nations (UN) was created in 1945** to replace the defunct League of Nations, which failed to prevent World War II. The intention and principles were first laid out in documents like the **Atlantic** **Charter (1941)**, which outlined the goals of the Allies post-war, including **collective security**. The **League of Nations** was created in June 1919, after World War I, as part of the **Treaty of Versailles**.
o **The term ‘United Nations’ was coined by President Roosevelt** to identify those countries which were allied against the axis powers – Germany, Italy and Japan. On January 1, 1942, representatives of 26 allied nations met in Washington DC to sign the declaration of the United Nations, which basically spelt out the war objectives of the Allied powers. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● The UN **did not establish the international court of Justice to try war crimes** from World War II. The **Nuremberg and Tokyo trials** were conducted by the victorious Allied Powers before the UN was fully operational and organized.
o The **International Court of Justice (ICJ)** is the principal judicial organ of the United Nations (UN) and established in June 1945 by the Charter of the United Nations. The seat of the Court is at the Peace Palace in The Hague (Netherlands). **Of the six principal organs of the United** **Nations, it is the only one not located in New York**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The United Nations came into existence on October 24, 1945, after being ratified by 51 nations, which included five permanent members (France, the Republic of China, the Soviet Union, the UK and the US) and 46 other signatories.
o **The four main goals of the UN included** maintaining international peace and security, developing friendly relations among nations, achieving international cooperation in solving international
problems and being at the center for harmonizing the actions of nations in the attainment of these common ends.
● At a high-level meeting of the **UN General Assembly in 2020, commemorating 75 years** of the UN, a declaration was adopted that also set out its goals for the next decade.
o In 2020, the UN General Assembly adopted the **"UN75 Declaration"** to mark its 75th anniversary,
which set out 12 commitments for the next decade. o The goals included protection of the planet and environment, promoting peace, gender equality and women empowerment, digital cooperation, and sustainable financing. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The United Nations (UN) completed 80 years in 2025 of its foundation.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
The term "Time Rondeau Crystal", frequently
seen in news, refers to:

options.a:
A specialized solid-state device used to
synchronize atomic clocks across vast geographical distances for satellite navigation systems
options.b:
A type of photonic crystal designed to slow
down light for use in advanced optical communication and quantum computing chips
options.c:
An ultra-hard composite crystal developed
using nanotechnology to coat aerospace components
options.d:
A state of matter whose component particles
exhibit a robust, repeating pattern of motion in time

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **Explanation**:
● **Recently scientists have discovered a new phase of matter called a time rondeau crystal** that shows an unusual kind of order in time, rather than in space like solids and liquids. The phase is related to, but distinct from, a time crystal.
○ In normal matter, the arrangement of atoms can be either ordered or disordered. For example, a (solid) crystal has a repeating structure while a liquid doesn’t. The order in a crystal comes from breaking the perfect symmetry of space: instead of looking the same everywhere, it repeats in a regular pattern.
● A **time crystal** is a new phase of matter that, unlike regular crystals, has a structure that repeats in time. When a system is periodically imparted some energy — like pushing a swing at regular intervals — it might oscillate at a different rhythm, such as every two pulses instead of one.
○ In a time crystal, the motion is perfectly regular, like a clock ticking at a fixed beat. **In a time** **rondeau crystal (TRC),** the system has a mix of order and randomness: it keeps a repeating pattern over long times, but between those repeating moments the motion is disordered or irregular. The researchers created a TRC using the spins of the nuclei of carbon-13 atoms in diamond. **So,** **option (d) is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Scientists have discovered a new phase of matter called the time rondeau crystal.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following best describes the
term Atomic Stencils?

options.a:
